topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed a monotonous, grating tune that seemed to amplify the silence in my chest. I watched Sarah and Mark walk toward the executive suite, their shared laughter echoing in the polished hallway. Every step they took felt like a deliberate upward trajectory, leaving me rooted to the linoleum floor. When my name was called for the review meeting, my hands started to tremble, a fine, almost invisible tremor that made gripping the folder feel like a physical strain. I rehearsed my opening statement in my head—a string of perfectly constructed, yet utterly hollow, accomplishments. When the decision finally came, the air seemed to thin. I nodded, the movement stiff, and felt a strange, heavy numbness spread through my limbs, as if the very capacity to feel anything significant had been temporarily suspended.

[story 2]

I spent the afternoon meticulously reorganizing the shared drive, a pointless exercise in control. The screen glowed harshly against my tired eyes, reflecting back a portrait of my own stagnation. I kept glancing at the glass partition separating my cubicle from the open-plan workspace where the promotion discussions were taking place. Sarah’s confidence, the way she leaned in, absorbing every word, contrasted sharply with my own internal paralysis. When the announcement finally broke, it wasn't a sharp sound, but a dull thud, like a heavy object dropping onto soft earth. I didn't look up immediately. Instead, I focused intently on a smudge on my monitor, tracing its outline with my gaze, a desperate attempt to anchor myself to something tangible when everything else felt like it was dissolving into mist.

[story 3]

The coffee tasted like ash this morning, bitter and undrinkable. I spent the entire morning staring at the condensation tracing slow paths down my water glass, observing the droplets merge and distort the view of the cityscape outside. The pressure was mounting; every casual interaction felt like a spotlight aimed directly at my inadequacy. When the opportunity was presented, my throat constricted. I managed a small, tight smile—a gesture that felt pasted on, brittle—and offered my prepared remarks. Hearing the affirmative response directed toward the other candidate felt like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. I simply gathered my papers, the sound of the rustling sound disproportionately loud in the quiet room, and walked toward the exit, my steps slow and heavy, as if moving through deep water.
